title: Past Simple
definition: The Past Simple tense describes actions that started and finished at a specific time in the past.

structure.positive: I visited Paris. / She played tennis.
structure.negative: I did not (didn't) visit Paris. / She did not play tennis.
structure.question: Did you visit Paris? / Did she play tennis?
structure.whQuestion: Where did you go? / What did she play?

usage: Use Past Simple for completed actions at a definite past time (I visited Paris last year), a sequence of past actions (He woke up, brushed his teeth, and left), and past habits (I played cricket every day as a child).
examples: I visited my grandmother last weekend.
She finished her homework before dinner.
They did not attend the meeting.
Did you watch the match yesterday?
We played football two hours ago.
I did not see him at the party.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Did you watch the cricket match yesterday?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Yes, I watched it with my friends.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: What did you do last weekend?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: I visited my hometown and met my family.

commonMistakes.why: Use the past tense form 'went', not the present form 'go', with a past time marker.
After 'did', use the base form of the verb, not the past form.
'Didn't' already carries the past meaning, so the main verb stays in base form.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
quiz.explanation: Use the simple past form 'went' for a completed past action.

quiz.question: She ___ (not/finish) her work last night.
quiz.explanation: Negative past simple: didn't + base verb.

quiz.question: Which signal word suggests Past Simple?
quiz.explanation: 'Yesterday' points to a specific finished time in the past.

quiz.sentence: Did she went to the market?
quiz.correction: Did she go to the market?
quiz.explanation: After 'did', always use the base form of the verb.
